lesson:
This is the second うまい in the list — the first (word 167) meant "good at, skilled." Same word, different face. うまい means both "skillful" and "tasty," and the connection isn't accidental. The original meaning was something like "satisfying" or "well-done" — food that's well-made tastes good, and a person who does things well is skilled. The word branched but never fully split.

In the "delicious" sense, うまい is casual and slightly masculine. このラーメン、うまい！ is something you'd hear at a ramen shop from a guy slurping noodles. It's earthier than おいしい, which is gender-neutral and appropriate everywhere. Women certainly use うまい too — the gender skew is real but not a rule.

The kanji 旨い exists but is rarely used. うまい almost always appears in hiragana, which keeps it looking informal — matching its casual register.

Dictionaryうまい
Negativeうまくない
Pastうまかった
Past negうまくなかった
Adverbうまく
て-formうまくて

思い (thought, feeling) + 出す (to take out, put out). You reach into your memory and pull a thought out. That's the physical metaphor: memories are stored inside you, and 思い出す is the act of extracting one. English says "recall" — to call back. Japanese says "think-out" — to push the thought outward into consciousness.

Don't confuse this with 覚おぼえる (to memorize). 覚える is putting information in. 思い出す is getting it back out. 単語たんごを覚おぼえる (memorize a word). 昔むかしのことを思おもい出だす (remember something from long ago). One is storage, the other is retrieval.

The noun form 思おもい出で — without the す — means "memory" or "recollection," always with a warm, nostalgic tinge. いい思おもい出で (a good memory). It's the word you'd use in a graduation speech or looking at old photos. The verb pulls the memory out; the noun is what you're left holding.

Particles: 思い出す takes を — the memory is the direct object you're extracting. 名前なまえを思おもい出だす (remember a name). 子供こどもの頃ころを思おもい出だす (remember childhood).

Potential が思おもい出だせる: が marks what you can recall — 名前なまえが思おもい出だせない (I can't remember the name). This negative potential form is extremely common — you use it when a memory is stuck on the tip of your tongue.

Passive に思おもい出だされる: rare. Memories being recalled is not typically framed as something done to you.

Causative に思おもい出ださせる: に marks who you remind. 彼女かのじょに昔むかしのことを思おもい出ださせた (I reminded her of the old days).

五段 Godan他動詞

Negativeおもいださないを
Politeおもいだしますを
Polite negおもいだしませんを
Pastおもいだしたを
Polite pastおもいだしましたを
て-formおもいだしてを
Volitionalおもいだそうを
Polite volおもいだしましょうを
Passiveおもいだされるに
Causativeおもいださせるに
Caus-passおもいださせられるに
Potentialおもいだせるが
Imperativeおもいだせを
Imp なさいおもいだしなさいを
Cond ～ばおもいだせばを
Cond ～たらおもいだしたらを

聞く is what you do on purpose — listen, ask. 聞こえる is what happens to you — sound reaches your ears whether you want it to or not. The difference is agency. 音楽おんがくを聞きく (listen to music — you chose to) vs 音楽おんがくが聞きこえる (music can be heard — it's just there). The が is your clue: the sound is the subject, acting on you.

This pairs with 見える (to be visible) — another involuntary perception verb. 見る/見える for sight, 聞く/聞こえる for sound. Active/passive perception. Japanese draws this line consistently where English often doesn't.

声こえが聞きこえる？ (can you hear my voice?) — the most common usage. You're asking if sound is reaching someone, not if they're trying to listen. 何なにも聞きこえない (I can't hear anything). 隣となりの部屋へやから音おとが聞きこえる (I can hear sounds from the next room).

Particles: 聞こえる takes が — the sound is the subject, not something you act on. 声こえが聞きこえる (a voice can be heard). Direction can be marked with から — 外そとから音おとが聞きこえる.

Passive: 聞こえる is already passive-like in meaning, so a separate passive form is essentially never used.

Causative に聞きこえさせる: extremely rare. If you want to "make something heard," you'd use 聞かせる (the causative of 聞く) instead.

English has "borrow" and "rent" as completely different concepts. Japanese doesn't care about the distinction. 借りる covers both — you're temporarily using something that belongs to someone else. 本ほんを借かりる (borrow a book). アパートを借かりる (rent an apartment). お金かねを借かりる (borrow money). Whether money changes hands is irrelevant to the Japanese word. What matters is that the thing goes back eventually.

Its partner is 貸かす (to lend). 借りる/貸す always describe the same transaction from opposite sides: I 借りる from you, you 貸す to me. Mixing them up is a classic mistake — just remember that 借りる is the receiver's verb.

The kanji 借 has the person radical (亻) on the left — a person doing the borrowing — and 昔 (the past, long ago) on the right, used here for its phonetic value. But there's an accidental poetry: borrowing links you to the past, because you owe something that has to come back.

Particles: 借りる takes を for the thing borrowed — 本ほんを借かりる. The person you borrow from takes に or から — 友達ともだちに借かりる or 友達ともだちから借かりる.

Potential が借かりられる: が — what you can borrow becomes the subject. ここでDVDが借かりられる (you can rent DVDs here).

Passive に借かりられる: に marks the borrower. 弟おとうとに車くるまを借かりられた (my brother borrowed my car — and I'm not happy about it).

Causative に…を借かりさせる: に marks who you make borrow. 子供こどもに図書館としょかんで本ほんを借かりさせる (have the kid borrow a book from the library).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 貸す (かす)

返す completes the circuit that 借りる starts. You borrowed it, now you give it back. 本ほんを返かえす (return a book). お金かねを返かえす (pay back money). The kanji 返 has the movement radical 辶 (road, movement) with 反 (turn, reverse) — something going back the way it came.

Don't confuse 返かえす with 帰かえる (to go home). Same sound family, different words. 返す is about returning things. 帰る is about returning yourself — going home. The す ending on 返す is your clue that it's transitive: you're acting on an object.

返す also shows up in compound verbs where it means "do back" or "reverse": 裏返うらがえす (turn inside out), 言いい返かえす (talk back, retort), 繰くり返かえす (repeat — turn over again). The 〜返す pattern always carries a sense of reversal or repetition.

Particles: 返す takes を for the thing returned — 本ほんを返かえす. The person you return it to takes に — 図書館としょかんに返かえす (return to the library).

Potential が返かえせる: が — what you can return becomes the subject. お金かねが返かえせない (I can't pay it back).

Passive に返かえされる: に marks who returned it. プレゼントを返かえされた (my present was returned to me — rejection).

Causative に…を返かえさせる: に marks who you make return something. 弟おとうとに本ほんを返かえさせる (make my brother return the book).

受け (receive, catch) + 取る (take). You receive and take — a double action that emphasizes physically getting your hands on something. This isn't abstract receiving like getting praise or an impression. 受け取る is concrete: 荷物にもつを受うけ取とる (pick up a package), 給料きゅうりょうを受うけ取とる (receive your salary), 手紙てがみを受うけ取とる (receive a letter).

There's also a figurative extension: to take something a certain way, to interpret. 冗談じょうだんとして受うけ取とる (take it as a joke). 悪わるく受うけ取とらないで (don't take it the wrong way). The physical metaphor still applies — you're "catching" the meaning in a particular way.

Neighborhood: もらう (receive from someone, with gratitude implied), 受ける (receive in a broader/more abstract sense — exams, impressions, surgery), 受け取る (physically receive and take possession). When a delivery person hands you a box, that's 受け取る.

Particles: 受け取る takes を for the thing received — 荷物にもつを受うけ取とる. The source takes から — 配達員はいたついんから荷物にもつを受うけ取とる (receive a package from the delivery person).

Potential が受うけ取とれる: が marks what you can receive. 今日中きょうじゅうに荷物にもつが受うけ取とれる (I can pick up the package today).

Passive に受うけ取とられる: に marks the receiver. 皮肉ひにくとして受うけ取とられた (it was taken as sarcasm).

Causative に受うけ取とらせる: に marks who you make receive. 部下ぶかに書類しょるいを受うけ取とらせる (have a subordinate pick up the documents).

The kanji 捨 is the hand radical (扌) plus 舎 — a building, a shelter. A hand pushing something out of the house. You're throwing it away, letting it go, done with it.

捨てる is blunt. It means to throw away, discard, dump — physically or metaphorically. ゴミを捨すてる (throw away trash). 古ふるい服ふくを捨すてる (toss old clothes). But it scales up: 夢ゆめを捨すてる (abandon a dream). 子供こどもを捨すてる (abandon a child). The harshness doesn't soften with abstract usage. If someone says 捨てられた about a relationship, it means they were discarded — tossed aside like garbage. It stings.

A useful set phrase: 捨すてたもんじゃない — literally "it's not something to throw away," meaning "it's not bad, it has value, don't dismiss it." A backhanded compliment structure. 日本にほんの冬ふゆも捨すてたもんじゃない (Japanese winter isn't so bad, actually).

Particles: 捨てる takes を — the thing you throw away is your direct object. ゴミを捨すてる. Where you throw it takes に — ゴミ箱ばこに捨すてる (throw it in the trash can).

Potential が捨すてられる: が — what you can throw away. 思おもい出では捨すてられない (I can't throw away the memories).

Passive に捨すてられる: に marks who did the discarding. 彼かれに捨すてられた (he dumped me). One of the more emotionally loaded passives in the language.

Causative に…を捨すてさせる: に marks who you make discard. 子供こどもにいらないおもちゃを捨すてさせる (make the kid throw away toys they don't need).

一 (one) + 緒 (thread, beginning). One thread — things bound together on a single line. The kanji 緒 has the thread radical (糸), reinforcing the image of connection.

The most common pattern is 一緒に — together, as in doing something alongside someone. 一緒いっしょに行いこう (let's go together). 一緒いっしょに食たべない？ (want to eat together?). This is the word you reach for when inviting someone to join you. It's warm and inclusive — the verbal equivalent of scooting over to make room.

But 一緒 also means "same": 私わたしと一緒いっしょだ (it's the same as me / it's the same as mine). 味あじは一緒いっしょ (the taste is the same). The two meanings — "together" and "same" — collapse into one idea: things that are together become the same, and things that are the same belong together.

The noun form of 遊あそぶ (to play). Strip off the ぶ, add nothing — 遊び is what you get when you nominalize the verb. Play itself. Fun as a thing.

Japanese 遊び has a wider range than English "play." Children play, yes — 子供こどもの遊あそび. But adults have 遊び too: going out, entertainment, leisure activities. 遊あそびに行いく (go out to have fun / go hang out) is how you describe meeting friends for no particular productive purpose. There's no childish connotation. It's the opposite of work.

遊び also has a mechanical meaning: slack, play, looseness in a machine or mechanism. ハンドルに遊あそびがある (there's play in the steering wheel). The conceptual bridge: play is the space where things aren't locked down tight, where there's room to move freely. Freedom of movement — whether it's kids in a park or a loose bolt — is 遊び.

移す means to move something from one place to another — a deliberate transfer. 荷物にもつを別べつの部屋へやに移うつす (move the luggage to another room). データを新あたらしいパソコンに移うつす (transfer data to a new computer). It implies purposeful relocation, not just general movement.

The kanji 移 has the grain radical (禾) on the left — think rice plants — and 多 (many) on the right. Transplanting seedlings, moving many plants from one paddy to another. Agriculture is the origin of this "transfer" meaning.

Pair: 移す (transitive — you move something) vs 移うつる (intransitive — something moves on its own). 会社かいしゃを東京とうきょうに移うつす (move the company to Tokyo — you're doing it) vs 会社かいしゃが東京とうきょうに移うつる (the company moves to Tokyo — it just happens). There's also a secondary meaning for both: spreading. 風邪かぜを移うつす (spread a cold to someone). 風邪かぜが移うつる (catch a cold from someone).

Particles: 移す takes を for the thing moved — 視線しせんを移うつす (shift one's gaze). Destination takes に.

Potential が移うつせる: が — what you can move. データが移うつせない (I can't transfer the data).

Passive に移うつされる: に marks who did the moving. 別べつの部署ぶしょに移うつされた (I was transferred to a different department).

Causative に移うつさせる: making someone transfer something. 部下ぶかにファイルを移うつさせる (have the subordinate move the files).

Take 大きい (big), drop the い, add さ — you get the abstract noun: bigness, size. This 〜さ suffix is one of the most useful tools in Japanese word-building. It turns any い-adjective into a measurable noun: 高い → 高さ (height), 長い → 長さ (length), 重い → 重さ (weight). Every time you see 〜さ on the end of a word, you're looking at a quality turned into a quantity.

大きさ is neutral — it doesn't mean "largeness." It means "size" in general. 大おおきさはどのくらい？ (how big is it? / what's the size?) can apply to anything from an ant to a building. 同おなじ大おおきさ (the same size). 手てのひらの大おおきさ (the size of a palm).

meaning: thought, idea, plan, way of thinking
lesson:
The noun form of 考かんがえる (to think deliberately). Where 考える is the verb — the process of working something out in your head — 考え is the product: the thought itself, the idea you arrived at, the plan you formed.

いい考かんがえだ (that's a good idea). 考かんがえが変かわった (my thinking changed / I changed my mind). あなたの考かんがえは？ (what do you think? — literally, what's your thought?). It can mean a deliberate plan: 何なにか考かんがえがあるの？ (do you have something in mind?).

Notice the difference from 思おもい, which is a feeling-thought, emotional and instinctive. 考え is a reasoning-thought, deliberate and analytical. The same split that separates the parent verbs 思う and 考える carries over into their noun forms. Your 考え is something you can explain and defend. Your 思い is something you feel and can't always articulate.

空 (sky, empty) + 港 (port, harbor). A port in the sky. Before airports existed, 港 meant a seaport — boats coming and going. When air travel arrived, Japanese simply swapped the element: sea-port became sky-port. The logic is clean and the word is instantly memorable once you see the pieces.

空港くうこうに着つく (arrive at the airport). 空港くうこうまで送おくる (take someone to the airport). 成田空港なりたくうこう (Narita Airport). In Japan, the airport name always comes first, then 空港: 羽田空港, 関西空港.

The kanji 港 has the water radical (氵) because it originally referred exclusively to water harbors. Combining it with 空 repurposes it for air travel — a nice example of how Japanese builds new vocabulary by recombining existing kanji rather than borrowing from English. (Though エアポート also exists as a loanword, 空港 is far more common.)

Every journey in Japanese starts with 出発 and ends with 到着とうちゃく (arrival). These are the two words on every airport display board, every train schedule, every bus timetable. 出発しゅっぱつの時間じかんだ (it's time to leave). 出発しゅっぱつが遅おくれた (the departure was delayed).

Both kanji push outward: 出 (go out) + 発 (emit, launch). The word has a kinetic quality that 出る alone doesn't — 出発 implies momentum, a deliberate setting-off. 九時くじに出発しゅっぱつする (depart at nine).

出発点しゅっぱつてん (starting point) extends the word into metaphor. ここが出発点しゅっぱつてんだ (this is where it all starts). A career, a project, a relationship — any beginning can be a 出発点. The word doesn't just mean leaving. It means launching toward something.

Before Google Maps, getting around in Japan required 地図 in a way most Western countries didn't experience. Japanese addresses aren't sequential — buildings aren't numbered in order along a street. A street address tells you the district, block, and building number, but finding the actual building often required a hand-drawn map. 地図ちずを書かいてください (please draw me a map) was once one of the most practical phrases in the language. Restaurants printed mini maps on their business cards. Wedding invitations included them.

The kanji: 地ち (earth, land) + 図ず (drawing, diagram). A drawing of the land. 地図ちずを見みる (look at a map). 世界地図せかいちず (world map). 地図ちずアプリ (map app).

図 shows up wherever something is being schematized: 図書館としょかん (library), 図形ずけい (shape/figure), 設計図せっけいず (blueprint). The kanji means "to map out" — whether it's land, a plan, or an idea.

運うん (carry, transport, luck) + 転てん (turn, roll, revolve). Carrying by turning — wheels turning, moving forward. The word covers the operation of any vehicle: 車くるまを運転うんてんする (drive a car), バスを運転うんてんする (drive a bus), 電車でんしゃを運転うんてんする (operate a train).

運転免許うんてんめんきょ (driver's license) — shortened in casual speech to just 免許めんきょ. 運転手うんてんしゅ (driver). 飲酒運転いんしゅうんてん (drunk driving). The word appears in compound after compound because driving is a central part of daily life.

Beyond vehicles, 運転 also means the operation of any machinery or system: 運転資金うんてんしきん (operating capital), 運転うんてんを再開さいかいする (resume operations — said of train lines after a delay). When a train line is disrupted, the announcement says 運転うんてんを見合みあわせています (operations are suspended).

meaning: to get off bus/train, go down, descend, land
lesson:
Getting on is 乗のる. Getting off is 降りる. These two bracket every ride you'll ever take in Japan. 次つぎの駅えきで降おりる (get off at the next station). バスを降おりる (get off the bus). 飛行機ひこうきが降おりる (the plane descends/lands).

The kanji 降 has the mound radical (阝) on the left — originally depicting a hill or earthen wall — and components suggesting downward movement on the right. Things come down: rain falls (降ふる, ふる), you step down (降りる). The shared kanji connects weather falling from the sky with people stepping down from vehicles — both are descents.

降りる vs 下りる: Both are read おりる and both mean "to descend." 降りる emphasizes getting off/out of something (vehicles, stages). 下おりる emphasizes going physically downward (stairs, mountains). In practice, the distinction is blurry and many Japanese speakers use them interchangeably.

Particles: 降りる takes を for the vehicle you exit — 電車でんしゃを降おりる (get off the train). This を marks the starting point of movement, not a direct object. Where you get off takes で — 渋谷しぶやで降おりる.

Passive に降おりられる: rare but possible. 途中とちゅうで客きゃくに降おりられた (the passenger got off on me mid-ride — a taxi driver's complaint).

Causative を／に降おりさせる: を for coercive — making someone get off. 酔よっ払ぱらいをタクシーから降おりさせる (make the drunk person get out of the taxi).

From English/Dutch "gas" — one of the older loanwords in Japanese, entering during the Meiji period when Western technology flooded in. In daily life, ガス almost always means cooking gas or heating gas — the stuff piped into your home. ガスをつける (turn on the gas). ガスを止とめる (shut off the gas). ガス代だい (gas bill).

In Japan, moving into a new apartment means calling three utility companies: 電気でんき (electricity), ガス (gas), and 水道すいどう (water). ガス is always in that trinity. ガス会社かいしゃ (gas company). ガスコンロ (gas stove). ガス漏もれ (gas leak) — a term seared into the national consciousness after the 1995 subway sarin attack, though that involved a different kind of gas entirely.

For gasoline (car fuel), Japanese uses ガソリン, not ガス. That's a common English speaker's mistake — asking for ガス at a gas station would be confusing.

必ず is a promise compressed into two syllables. It doesn't mean "always" in the statistical sense (いつも handles that). It means "without fail" — a guarantee. 必かならず来きてね (make sure you come). 必かならず成功せいこうする (it will definitely succeed). 約束やくそくは必かならず守まもる (I always keep my promises — not as a habit, as a commitment).

The kanji 必 looks like a heart (心) pierced by a line — something driven through the center with absolute certainty. The character means "inevitable, certain, without fail" and shows up in compounds that share that iron certainty: 必要ひつよう (necessary), 必死ひっし (desperate — literally "certain death").

In practice, 必ず often appears in instructions and warnings: 必かならず確認かくにんしてください (be sure to check). It's the word that turns a suggestion into an imperative without raising your voice.

From English "camera," but the word has been in Japanese long enough to feel fully naturalized. カメラ covers every kind: デジタルカメラ (digital camera, shortened to デジカメ), ビデオカメラ (video camera), 防犯ぼうはんカメラ (security camera).

Japan's camera industry shaped the global market. Canon, Nikon, Sony, Fujifilm, Olympus — all Japanese. カメラマン means "photographer" or "cameraman" in Japanese (another loanword compound). The word is deeply embedded in a country where photography is practically a national pastime — cherry blossom season turns every park into a sea of cameras.

The phone camera has shifted the word's center of gravity. カメラを向むける (point a camera at someone) used to mean a physical device; now it often means a phone. カメラで撮とる (take a photo with a camera) is giving way to スマホで撮とる. But カメラ remains the default word for the device itself.

meaning: to commute, go regularly, frequent a place
lesson:
通う is about regular trips back and forth — a path you travel repeatedly. Not a one-time journey (that's 行く), but a routine. 学校がっこうに通かよう (go to school — every day, as a student). 病院びょういんに通かよう (go to the hospital regularly — for ongoing treatment). ジムに通かよう (go to the gym regularly). The word implies commitment: you're not visiting once, you're making it part of your life.

The kanji 通 is the movement radical (辶) with 甬 — a passageway. Something that goes through, passes through, connects. It shows up in 通とおる (to pass through), 通とおり (street), 交通こうつう (traffic). The difference: 通る is a single passage, 通う is the repeated version — going through the same passage again and again.

Particles: 通う takes に for the destination — the place you go to regularly. 塾じゅくに通かよう (attend cram school).

Passive に通かよわれる: rare, but can appear in honorific speech — 先生せんせいはこの道場どうじょうに通かよわれていた (the teacher used to attend this dojo — respectful).

Causative を／に通かよわせる: を for coercive, に for permissive. 子供こどもを塾じゅくに通かよわせる (have the kid attend cram school) — one of the most commonly heard uses of this causative in Japan.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryかように
Negativeかよわないに
Politeかよいますに
Polite negかよいませんに
Pastかよったに
Polite pastかよいましたに
て-formかよってに
Volitionalかよおうに
Polite volかよいましょうに
Passiveかよわれるに
Causativeかよわせるを／に
Caus-passかよわせられるに
Potentialかよえるに
Imperativeかよえに
Imp なさいかよいなさいに
Cond ～ばかよえばに
Cond ～たらかよったらに

急 means "sudden, urgent, steep" — and に turns it into an adverb. 急きゅうに雨あめが降ふってきた (it suddenly started raining). 急きゅうに寒さむくなった (it got cold all of a sudden). The word captures a change that arrives without warning — one moment things are normal, the next they're different.

The kanji 急 has 心 (heart) at the bottom and components suggesting grabbing or pressing at the top. Something seizing your heart — that jolt when circumstances shift without preparation. It appears in 急行きゅうこう (express train), 緊急きんきゅう (emergency), 急いそぐ (to hurry). Speed, urgency, abruptness — always that sense of no time to spare.

急に vs 突然とつぜん: both mean "suddenly" but 突然 is more dramatic, more literary. 急に is the everyday version — something changed fast. 突然 is for when the door slams open in a horror movie.

"Salary" + "man" — a Japanese-English compound (和製英語) that doesn't exist in actual English. It means a white-collar office worker, typically male, employed full-time at a company. The word carries an entire lifestyle: long hours, commuter trains, dark suits, after-work drinking with colleagues, loyalty to one company for an entire career. Whether that image is admired or pitied depends on who's talking.

The term has become somewhat loaded. It can be neutral — 父ちちはサラリーマンです (my father is an office worker) — or it can imply a faceless cog in the corporate machine, someone who traded individuality for stability. Japanese media often contrasts サラリーマン with entrepreneurs or freelancers as a way of questioning traditional career paths.

For women, the equivalent was OL (office lady), though that term is increasingly seen as outdated and reductive. The gender-neutral 会社員かいしゃいん (company employee) is the safer, more formal alternative for any gender.

In Japan, the 25th of the month is 給料日きゅうりょうび (payday) for most workers. Restaurants near office districts get busier that evening. The phrase 給料日前きゅうりょうびまえ (before payday) is shorthand for being broke — those last few days when the wallet is thin and lunch options narrow.

給 (supply) + 料 (fee): what's supplied to you for your work. 給料きゅうりょうが出でる (get paid — literally "salary comes out"). 給料きゅうりょうが安やすい (the pay is low). Talking about money openly is still somewhat taboo in Japan, but 給料 is the word people reach for when they do.

Neighborhood: 給料 is everyday. 給与きゅうよ is formal — tax documents and HR departments. 月給げっきゅう is monthly salary specifically. 年収ねんしゅう is annual income. The more formal the word, the less likely you are to hear it in conversation.

meaning: piece of music, tune, song, melody
lesson:
The kanji 曲 originally meant "bent, curved" — and it still carries that meaning in 曲まがる (to turn/bend). How did "bent" become "song"? The connection is the winding, turning quality of melody — a tune doesn't go straight, it curves up and down, bends through intervals. A piece of music is a curve of sound.

この曲きょくが好すき (I like this song). 新曲しんきょく (new song/track). 名曲めいきょく (masterpiece/famous piece). 曲 also serves as a counter for songs: 三曲さんきょく (three songs/tracks).

Note that 曲 and 歌うた (song) overlap but aren't identical. 歌 emphasizes singing — a song with vocals. 曲 is broader — any piece of music, including instrumentals. A Beethoven symphony is a 曲 but not really a 歌. A pop song is both.

meaning: to be cut, break, run out, cut well
lesson:
切れる is the intransitive partner of 切きる (to cut). Where 切る is you cutting something, 切れる is something getting cut, breaking, or running out — on its own, without your hand on the blade.

The word branches widely. 紐ひもが切きれた (the string broke). 電池でんちが切きれた (the battery died — ran out). 期限きげんが切きれた (the deadline expired). このナイフはよく切きれる (this knife cuts well). That last usage is different — "cuts well" describes the knife's ability, not something breaking. Context always tells you which 切れる you're dealing with.

There's also an idiomatic sense: 頭あたまが切きれる (sharp-minded, clever) — a mind that cuts through problems. And キレる (written in katakana) means "to snap, lose one's temper" — a slang usage that became mainstream. The thread connecting all these: a sharp break, a clean separation.

Particles: 切れる takes が — the thing that breaks or runs out is the subject. 電池でんちが切きれた. パンが切きれている (we're out of bread).

Passive: Already intransitive, so passive is essentially unused.

Causative を／に切きれさせる: rare. You'd normally use 切る (transitive) instead of forcing 切れる into a causative.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 切る (きる)
Dictionaryきれるが
Negativeきれないが
Politeきれますが
Polite negきれませんが
Pastきれたが
Polite pastきれましたが
て-formきれてが
Volitionalきれようが
Polite volきれましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばきれればが
Cond ～たらきれたらが

The kanji 正 is 一 (one) over 止 (stop). The original meaning: to go straight toward a goal without deviating — to stop at the one correct place. From there: correct, right, proper, just. It's one of those kanji whose meaning is so foundational it appears everywhere: 正月 (New Year), 正午 (noon), 正式 (formal/official), 正直 (honest).

正しい covers both factual correctness and moral rightness. 正ただしい答こたえ (the correct answer). 正ただしい判断はんだん (the right decision). 正ただしいことをする (do the right thing). English needs two different words — "correct" for facts, "righteous" for morality — but Japanese uses one. The assumption baked into the word: truth and goodness are the same thing.

The sound ta-da-shi-i is long — four morae — which gives it a weighty, authoritative feel in speech. When someone says 正しい, it sounds like a verdict being delivered.

Dictionaryただしい
Negativeただしくない
Pastただしかった
Past negただしくなかった
Adverbただしく
て-formただしくて

苦しい is physical and emotional pain at the same time. 息いきが苦くるしい (it's hard to breathe). 生活せいかつが苦くるしい (life is hard / financially tight). 苦くるしい言いい訳わけ (a strained excuse). The word describes the feeling of being squeezed — something pressing on you that you can't escape.

The kanji 苦 has the plant radical (艹) on top — it originally referred to a bitter-tasting plant. Bitterness extended to suffering: the taste of hardship. 苦にがい still means "bitter" for tastes, while 苦しい is the suffering version. Same kanji, two different adjective forms, two related but distinct meanings.

苦しい vs 痛いたい (painful): 痛い is sharp, localized pain — you stubbed your toe, your head hurts. 苦しい is prolonged, whole-body distress — you can't breathe, you're struggling, the weight is too much. 痛い makes you flinch. 苦しい makes you endure.

Dictionaryくるしい
Negativeくるしくない
Pastくるしかった
Past negくるしくなかった
Adverbくるしく
て-formくるしくて

細かい lives in the space between "small" and "detailed." Small change: 細こまかいお金かね. Fine print: 細こまかい字じ. Detailed instructions: 細こまかい説明せつめい. A meticulous person: 細こまかい人ひと. The connecting idea: things broken down into tiny, precise pieces.

That last usage — 細かい人 — is a double-edged compliment. It can mean someone who's thorough and detail-oriented, or someone who's fussy and nitpicky. そんな細こまかいこと気きにしないで (don't worry about such minor details) leans negative. 細こまかいところまで気きが付つく (notices even the fine details) leans positive. Context and tone decide.

The kanji 細 has the thread radical (糸) — something thin and fine. 細ほそい means physically thin or slender. 細かい means small/detailed. Both share the kanji because both involve fineness — thin things and tiny things are cousins.

Dictionaryこまかい
Negativeこまかくない
Pastこまかかった
Past negこまかくなかった
Adverbこまかく
て-formこまかくて

The kanji 静 is 青 (blue/green) + 争 (conflict, dispute). An odd combination — conflict plus a color? The folk reading: when the fighting stops and everything settles into a clear blue calm, that's 静. Peace after conflict. Stillness after noise. Whether or not that's historically accurate, the image works: the blue sky after a storm.

静しずかにしてください (please be quiet) — you will hear this in libraries, on trains, from parents to children. It's the polite way to ask for silence. 静しずかな朝あさ (a quiet morning). 静しずかに暮くらす (live quietly). The word covers both literal silence and a calm, undisturbed state of being.

静か vs うるさい (noisy, annoying) — direct opposites that Japanese people toggle between constantly. A 静かな neighborhood is desirable. うるさい neighbors are a problem. The contrast is binary in a way that reflects Japanese sensitivity to noise — shared walls, thin houses, crowded trains make quietness a social virtue.

Dictionaryしずかだ
Negativeしずかじゃない
Pastしずかだった
Past negしずかじゃなかった
Adverbしずかに
て-formしずかで

健 (robust, healthy) + 康 (peaceful, at ease). A body that's strong and at ease — health. The word works as both a noun and a な-adjective: 健康けんこうが大事だいじ (health is important) and 健康けんこうな体からだ (a healthy body).

Japan is obsessed with 健康 in a way that shapes daily life. 健康診断けんこうしんだん (health checkup) is mandatory for most workers once a year. 健康食品けんこうしょくひん (health foods) fill entire aisles in convenience stores. TV shows dedicate segments to 健康法 (health methods). The word isn't abstract — it's a daily pursuit with specific rituals: morning walks, balanced meals, regular checkups, hot baths.

A common greeting: お体からだに気きをつけて (take care of yourself — literally, be careful with your body). Health isn't just a personal matter in Japan. Wishing someone good health is a social act, especially toward elders. 健康 is what you wish for others and work toward for yourself.

Dictionaryけんこうだ
Negativeけんこうじゃない
Pastけんこうだった
Past negけんこうじゃなかった
Adverbけんこうに
て-formけんこうで

From English "golf." The sport arrived in Japan in 1903 and became a corporate religion. ゴルフ in Japan is less a weekend hobby than a business ritual — playing a round with clients or superiors is a career move. 接待せったいゴルフ (business entertaining golf) is a compound word that exists because the practice is so common it needed a name.

That said, golf's role has been shifting. Course membership fees that once cost millions of yen have dropped. Younger Japanese are less drawn to the corporate golf culture. But the word itself remains common: ゴルフ場じょう (golf course), ゴルフクラブ (golf club — both the equipment and the organization).

ゴルフをする or ゴルフに行く — both are natural. The first emphasizes playing, the second emphasizes going. If someone invites you with 今度こんどゴルフ行いきませんか, they're suggesting an outing, not just a game.

From English "course," but Japanese uses it more broadly than you might expect. マラソンコース (marathon course/route). ゴルフコース (golf course). ランチコース (lunch course — a set meal with multiple dishes). That last one catches English speakers off guard: a コース at a restaurant means a prix fixe meal, not a main course.

コース料理りょうり (course meal, full-course dinner) is the restaurant usage you'll encounter most. Aコース and Bコース on a menu mean "set A" and "set B" — different packages at different price points. The word implies a structured sequence: appetizer, main, dessert, flowing one after another.

In education: 文系ぶんけいコース (humanities track), 理系りけいコース (science track). In life planning: 人生じんせいのコース (life course/path). The word always implies a predetermined route or sequence — something laid out for you to follow.

meaning: to request, ask, beg, order (food/drink), entrust
lesson:
頼む is the everyday word for asking someone to do something for you. Not asking a question (聞く), not requesting formally (お願いする) — just asking, person to person: can you do this? 友達ともだちに頼たのむ (ask a friend). 仕事しごとを頼たのむ (ask someone to do work).

At restaurants, 頼む means "to order": ビールを頼たのむ (order a beer). It's more casual than 注文ちゅうもんする, which is the formal "place an order." Between friends at an izakaya, もう一杯いっぱい頼たのもう (let's order another) is natural.

The one-word exclamation 頼む！ (or 頼むよ) means "I'm begging you" or "come on, please" — the kind of thing you say when you really need someone to come through. 頼たのむから静しずかにして (I'm begging you, please be quiet). It's direct, slightly desperate, and very human.

The kanji 頼 has 束 (bundle) + 頁 (head/page). The idea of bowing your head in a bundle of obligation — relying on someone. 頼たよる (to rely on) shares the same kanji.

Particles: 頼む takes を for the thing requested and に for the person asked. 友達ともだちに引ひっ越こしを頼たのむ (ask a friend to help move). At a restaurant, just を: ビールを頼たのむ.

Potential が頼たのめる: が marks what you can order/request. ここでランチが頼たのめる (you can order lunch here).

Passive に頼たのまれる: に marks who asked you. 上司じょうしに頼たのまれた (my boss asked me to do it). Very common — being asked to do things by others is a constant.

Causative に頼たのませる: making someone request. Uncommon — you'd normally just make the request yourself.
